does: This paper introduces Ethical-Lens, a framework that enhances the ethical alignment of open-source text-to-image models without modifying the underlying model weights. The approach works by refining user prompts before they reach the model and post-processing generated images to remove problematic content. The framework targets two key dimensions: toxicity and bias. It uses a combination of evaluation metrics—GPT-4V for toxicity assessment, HEIM for fairness, and FairFace for demographic bias—to systematically measure alignment improvements. Experimental results demonstrate that Ethical-Lens achieves alignment performance comparable to or exceeding commercial models like DALLE 3, while preserving image quality. The method enables open-source model deployment with ethical safeguards applied at inference time rather than through costly model retraining.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research addressing safety and alignment in generative models, extending existing prompt refinement and output filtering techniques from the language model domain to vision synthesis. It responds to concerns raised in studies examining bias and toxicity in text-to-image systems, and it complements concurrent work on constitutional AI and value alignment methods. The approach acknowledges limitations of fine-tuning and RLHF-based alignment strategies, instead leveraging external classifiers and repair mechanisms similar to those explored in content moderation and bias mitigation literature. The paper also engages with debates around whether alignment should be achieved through model architecture versus external safeguards, positioning itself within the emerging paradigm of deployable safety interventions for foundation models.
enables: This framework opens pathways for making open-source text-to-image models safely deployable in high-stakes contexts where ethical alignment is required but model retraining is impractical. It suggests that effective governance of generative models need not require closed-source approaches or internal modifications, potentially democratizing responsible AI deployment. Future work could extend the approach to additional harm dimensions beyond toxicity and bias, develop more sophisticated prompt engineering and image repair techniques, or investigate how such external alignment layers interact with adversarial attempts to circumvent them. The methodology also points toward broader strategies for retrofitting safety guarantees onto existing foundation models without retraining, which could inform approaches to alignment in emerging multimodal and large-scale systems.
